tagline: Online resharding lands, replicas get put on probation, and Postgres remains enough.
editionNote: One Helm release, two heavyweight PRs, and a philosophical declaration about database sufficiency.

articles.headline: helm v0.45 ships with a fix nobody asked @levkk for
articles.deck: A community contributor upgrades the ExternalSecret API version, and the Helm chart gets a fresh tag.
articles.body: helm v0.45 landed on February 25 with a single but meaningful change: switching to the v1 ExternalSecret apiVersion. The patch came from first-time contributor @mikkelam. Not every release needs fireworks. Sometimes the plumbing just needs a new fitting.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a small brass valve being carefully turned on a massive network of gleaming pipes

articles.headline: pgdog learns to reshard a live database without pulling the plug
articles.deck: Online resharding arrives in pgdog, letting operators rebalance data across shards without downtime.
articles.body: The headliner this week is #784 Online resharding, merged by @levkk. This gives pgdog the ability to move data between shards while the system stays up and serving queries. Accompanying documentation also shipped: #55 feat: document new reshard command and schema sync and #56 Document cutover landed in docs the same week. The feature arrived with its own manual. Rare courtesy.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a conveyor belt smoothly transferring glowing data cubes between two towering warehouse shelves

articles.headline: Slow replicas now get benched automatically
articles.deck: Replica lag banning plus a batch of bug fixes traced back to Hacker News feedback.
articles.body: @levkk merged #788 replica lag banning, multiple bug fixes from HN, which introduces automatic banning of replicas that fall behind on replication. The PR also bundles several bug fixes surfaced by the Hacker News crowd. Nothing like a front-page post to shake loose the edge cases.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a referee blowing a whistle at a row of database cylinders, one slumping on a bench

articles.headline: docs catches up to the resharding train
articles.deck: Two PRs document cutover procedures and the new reshard command before users have to guess.
articles.body: @levkk merged #55 feat: document new reshard command and schema sync and #56 Document cutover in the docs repo. The reshard command, schema sync behavior, and cutover procedures all got written up. Five open issues remain on the backlog, including one to generate an LLM-friendly text dump at /llms.txt. The machines want docs too, apparently.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a technical writer furiously stamping pages as they fly off a printing press onto a moving train

articles.headline: Wildcard database templates waiting at the door
articles.deck: A community PR proposes letting pgdog match databases by pattern instead of explicit config.
articles.body: Contributor @hikionori has #794 feat: add wildcard database template support open against pgdog. If merged, operators could define database connection templates using wildcards rather than listing every database individually. The PR is still under review.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a hotel concierge holding a skeleton key in front of an endless hallway of numbered doors

articles.headline: pgdog joins the "Postgres is enough" registry
articles.deck: @levkk adds pgdog to the curated list of tools that bet everything on Postgres.
articles.body: @levkk merged #2 feat: add PgDog into postgresisenough, the community-maintained catalog of projects built on the conviction that Postgres handles more than you think. One open issue notes the auth tag is misbehaving. Philosophical certainty, minor technical uncertainty.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a single elephant standing confidently atop a mountain of discarded technology logos

closingNote: Printed on recycled query plans. Back next Monday, assuming the replicas keep up.
